query: javascript event bubbling and capturing interview question

competitors.label: MDN Event bubbling
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical frontend docs source for event propagation, bubbling, capture, and delegation-related behavior.
competitors.winReason: Our page now answers this interview question with two concrete bug families at once: nested modal/backdrop clicks and delegated menu handling, plus one combined propagation trace.
theirStrengths: It covers target/currentTarget, stopPropagation, and delegation-oriented follow-ups in one place.
theirGaps: It is less interview-oriented than our shipped trivia.

realWorldUseCases: When an event (like a click) occurs in the DOM, it does not belong to only one element. It travels through a three-phase path: capture -> target -> bubble. The real production/debug value is understanding why a nested button can trigger its panel and backdrop, why delegated menus sometimes read the wrong node, and why blindly calling stopPropagation() often hides the wrong bug.
actionableExamples: shell capture -> menu capture -> item target -> menu bubble -> shell bubble

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers nested events and propagation well, but our page is more directly framed around concrete modal and delegated-menu bugs.
actionableExamples: MDN still has strong runnable examples, yet our single combined trace plus delegated-menu snippet is tighter for this interview-sized page.
followUpConfusion: MDN remains broader on related propagation details, so the follow-up comparison stays close.
Add one note on retargeting behavior across web components if we want a clearer advanced edge beyond composedPath().

competitors.label: javascript.info Bubbling and capturing
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a strong tutorial reference for propagation phases and common propagation controls.
competitors.winReason: javascript.info explains the phases well, but our page now wins this comparison by pairing a combined phase trace with a tighter follow-up set around delegated handlers, stopPropagation misuse, and composedPath().
theirStrengths: It explains propagation phases with practical examples and clearly shows how handlers fire.
theirGaps: It is less concise for rapid interview recall than our trivia format.

followUpConfusion: If a component uses shadow DOM, composedPath() can reveal the full path more accurately than guessing from one node.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info still has a strong practical explanation of nested DOM propagation, so the real-world criterion remains close.
actionableExamples: Its examples are solid, but our page now adds one combined trace and one delegated-menu snippet tailored to UI debugging.
followUpConfusion: Our follow-up section now answers stopPropagation misuse and shadow-DOM path questions more directly.

realWorldUseCases: Add one outside-click listener example if we want a stronger real-world edge than the current modal plus delegated-menu set.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Event phases
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep answer focused on the browser event phases.
competitors.winReason: Our page now goes beyond the phase definition by grounding the answer in modal/backdrop bugs, a single propagation trace, and a delegated-menu example that matches real UI debugging better.
theirStrengths: It is closely matched to the interview phrasing and stays focused on the three event phases.
theirGaps: It appears lighter on concrete nested-DOM examples than longer tutorial sources.

realWorldUseCases: When an event (like a click) occurs in the DOM, it does not belong to only one element. It travels through a three-phase path: capture -> target -> bubble. The real production/debug value is understanding why a nested button can trigger its panel and backdrop, why delegated menus sometimes read the wrong node, and why blindly calling stopPropagation() often hides the wrong bug.
GreatFrontEnd explains the phase order clearly, but it is lighter on the concrete bug framing around modals and delegated handlers.
actionableExamples: Its examples are concise, while our page now includes both the phase trace and the delegated-menu implementation.
followUpConfusion: GreatFrontEnd still answers the core phase-order follow-up well enough that the broader follow-up comparison stays close.
Add one quick note on which native events do not bubble if we want a broader advanced follow-up set.
